title: evanverse
text: evanverse 目前正在积极维护中。CRAN 版本已被归档，v0.5.2 修复完成后将重新提交。 开发版可通过 GitHub 安装：
code: devtools::install_github("evanbio/evanverse")

title: evanverse
text: evanverse 是一个 R 工具包，提供约 50 个函数，涵盖包管理、数据可视化、统计检验、生物信息学等领域。由 Evan Zhou 开发。
code: library(evanverse)  "你好" %p% "世界"                      # 字符串拼接 set_mirror("cran", "tuna")            # 配置 CRAN 镜像 plot_venn(list(A = 1:5, B = 3:8))    # 快速绘制韦恩图 quick_ttest(df, "group", "value")     # 自动选择 t 检验方法 gene2ensembl(c("TP53", "BRCA1"))      # 基因 ID 转换

title: evanverse
text: 系统要求： R ≥ 4.1.0
code: # 稳定版（CRAN） install.packages("evanverse")  # 开发版 devtools::install_github("evanbio/evanverse")

title: evanverse
text: pkg_functions() — 列出包中的函数 set_mirror() — 配置 CRAN / Bioconductor 镜像 说明： inst_pkg()、check_pkg()、update_pkg() 和 pkg_version() 已从本包移除。我们发现 pak 提供了更加优雅、高性能的包管理工作流——并行安装、统一的 pak::pkg_install() 接口（支持 CRAN / GitHub / Bioconductor），并内置状态查询与更新功能。推荐直接使用 pak。 plot_venn() — 韦恩图 plot_forest() — 森林图 plot_bar() — 柱状图 plot_pie() — 饼图 plot_density() — 密度图 quick_ttest() — 自动方法选择的 t 检验 quick_anova() — 单因素方差分析（含事后检验） quick_chisq() — 卡方 / Fisher 精确检验 quick_cor() — 相关性分析（含热力图） stat_power() — 统计功效分析 stat_n() — 样本量计算 配色方案功能已迁移至独立包 biopalette。 安装方式：devtools::install_github("evanbio/biopalette") download_url() — 下载单个 URL download_batch() — 批量下载 download_geo() — 下载 GEO 数据集 file_info() — 文件元信息 file_ls() — 列出文件及元信息 file_tree() — 目录树可视化 view() — 交互式数据查看 gene2ensembl() — 基因符号转 Ensembl ID gene2entrez() — 基因符号转 Entrez ID download_gene_ref() — 下载基因参考表 gmt2df() — GMT 解析为数据框 gmt2list() — GMT 解析为列表 df2list() — 数据框转命名列表 df2vect() — 数据框列转命名向量 recode_column() — 按映射表重编码列值 %p% — 字符串拼接 %% — 身份比较 %nin% — 非成员判断 %map% — 映射操作符 %match% — 匹配操作符 comb() — 组合数 perm() — 排列数 toy_gene_ref() — 用于测试的小型基因参考表 toy_gmt() — 用于测试的小型 GMT 文件

title: Base Utilities
text: base module provides thirteen utility functions covering six areas:
code: library(evanverse)

what: df2list() — Split a data frame into a named list
title: Base Utilities
text: Groups one column’s values another column returns named list. Useful building marker lists, gene set inputs, grouping operation downstream functions expect list.
code: df <- data.frame(   cell_type = c("T_cell", "T_cell", "B_cell", "B_cell", "B_cell"),   marker    = c("CD3D", "CD3E", "CD79A", "MS4A1", "CD19"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  df2list(df, group_col = "cell_type", value_col = "marker") #> $T_cell #> [1] "CD3D" "CD3E" #> #> $B_cell #> [1] "CD79A" "MS4A1" "CD19"

what: df2vect() — Extract a named vector from a data frame
title: Base Utilities
text: Extracts two columns returns named vector, using one column names values. original value type preserved. name column must contain NA, empty strings, duplicates — three caught input raise informative error.
code: df <- data.frame(   gene  = c("TP53", "BRCA1", "MYC"),   score = c(0.91, 0.74, 0.55),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  df2vect(df, name_col = "gene", value_col = "score") #>  TP53 BRCA1   MYC #>  0.91  0.74  0.55 bad <- data.frame(id = c("a", "a"), val = 1:2) df2vect(bad, "id", "val") #> Error in `df2vect()`: #> ! `name_col` contains duplicate values.

what: recode_column() — Map column values via a named vector
title: Base Utilities
text: Replaces values column using named vector (dict). Unmatched values receive scalar default (NA default). Set name write new column instead overwriting source. Explicit NA values dict kept matched values rather replaced default.
code: df <- data.frame(   gene = c("TP53", "BRCA1", "EGFR", "XYZ"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  dict <- c("TP53" = "Tumour suppressor", "EGFR" = "Oncogene")  # Overwrite in place recode_column(df, column = "gene", dict = dict) #>                gene #> 1 Tumour suppressor #> 2              <NA> #> 3          Oncogene #> 4              <NA>  # Write to a new column, keep original; use a custom fallback recode_column(df, column = "gene", dict = dict,               name = "role", default = "Unknown") #>    gene              role #> 1  TP53 Tumour suppressor #> 2 BRCA1           Unknown #> 3  EGFR          Oncogene #> 4   XYZ           Unknown

title: Base Utilities
text: Returns interactive reactable widget search, filtering, sorting, pagination. RStudio widget renders Viewer pane; environments renders default HTML output. view() requires reactable package. installed, function raises clear error rather falling back silently.
code: view(iris, n = 10)

title: Base Utilities
text: Returns data frame file metadata files directory. Columns: file, size_MB, modified_time, path.
code: # All files in the current directory file_ls(".") #>              file size_MB       modified_time                          path #> 1  DESCRIPTION   0.002  2026-03-20 14:22:01  F:/project/evanverse/DESCRIPTION #> 2    NAMESPACE   0.002  2026-03-20 14:22:01  F:/project/evanverse/NAMESPACE #> ...  # R source files only, searched recursively file_ls("R", recursive = TRUE, pattern = "\\.R$")

title: Base Utilities
text: Returns four-column data frame file_ls() explicit vector file paths rather directory scan. Duplicate paths input silently deduplicated. Missing files raise error listing unresolved paths.
code: file_info(c("DESCRIPTION", "NAMESPACE")) #>          file size_MB       modified_time                          path #> 1 DESCRIPTION   0.002  2026-03-20 14:22:01  F:/project/evanverse/DESCRIPTION #> 2   NAMESPACE   0.002  2026-03-20 14:22:01  F:/project/evanverse/NAMESPACE

title: Base Utilities
text: Prints directory structure tree format. Returns lines invisibly output can captured needed.
code: file_tree(".", max_depth = 2) #> F:/project/evanverse #> +-- DESCRIPTION #> +-- NAMESPACE #> +-- R #> |   +-- base.R #> |   +-- plot.R #> |   +-- utils.R #> +-- tests #>     +-- testthat

title: Base Utilities
text: gene2entrez() gene2ensembl() accept character vector gene symbols return three-column data frame: original input (symbol), case-normalised form used matching (symbol_std), converted ID.

title: Base Utilities
text: Matching performed ref data frame columns symbol, entrez_id, ensembl_id. Two sources available:
code: # Fast, offline reference for development ref <- toy_gene_ref(species = "human")  # Full reference for analysis (requires network + Bioconductor) # ref <- download_gene_ref(species = "human")

title: Base Utilities
text: Unmatched symbols returned NA ID column rather dropped. reference table contains duplicated symbols case normalisation, first match used warning emitted.

title: Base Utilities
code: ref <- toy_gene_ref(species = "human")  gene2entrez(c("tp53", "BRCA1", "GHOST"), ref = ref, species = "human") #>   symbol symbol_std entrez_id #> 1   tp53       TP53      7157 #> 2  BRCA1      BRCA1       672 #> 3  GHOST      GHOST      <NA>

title: Base Utilities
code: ref_mouse <- toy_gene_ref(species = "mouse")  gene2ensembl(c("Trp53", "TRP53", "FakeGene"), ref = ref_mouse, species = "mouse") #>     symbol symbol_std          ensembl_id #> 1    Trp53      trp53  ENSMUSG00000059552 #> 2    TRP53      trp53  ENSMUSG00000059552 #> 3 FakeGene   fakegene                <NA>

title: Base Utilities
text: GMT (Gene Matrix Transposed) standard format gene set collections MSigDB. line encodes one gene set: term, description, tab-separated list gene symbols. toy_gmt() writes minimal GMT file temp path offline use:
code: tmp <- toy_gmt(n = 3) readLines(tmp) #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY\tGenes regulated by p53\tTP53\tBRCA1\tMYC\t..." #> [2] "HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING\tGenes upregulated by mTORC1\tPTEN\t..." #> [3] "HALLMARK_HYPOXIA\tGenes upregulated under hypoxia\tMTOR\tHIF1A\t..."

title: Base Utilities
text: Returns one row per gene, making output directly compatible dplyr data.table workflows.
code: df <- gmt2df(tmp) head(df, 4) #>                      term               description  gene #> 1   HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY  Genes regulated by p53   TP53 #> 2   HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY  Genes regulated by p53  BRCA1 #> 3   HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY  Genes regulated by p53    MYC #> 4   HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY  Genes regulated by p53   EGFR

what: gmt2list() — Named list of gene vectors
title: Base Utilities
text: Returns named list element character vector gene symbols. format expected gene set enrichment tools (e.g., fgsea, clusterProfiler). Lines fewer 3 tab-separated fields skipped warning removed result. every line malformed, functions raise error valid gene set can returned.
code: gs <- gmt2list(tmp) names(gs) #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY"      "HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING" #> [3] "HALLMARK_HYPOXIA"  gs[["HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY"]] #>  [1] "TP53"   "BRCA1"  "MYC"    "EGFR"   "PTEN"   "CDK2"   "MDM2" #>  [8] "RB1"    "CDKN2A" "AKT1"

title: Base Utilities
text: Calculates number ordered arrangements k items n distinct items: perm(n, 0) returns 1. k > n, result 0.

title: Base Utilities
text: Calculates number ways choose k items n distinct items: comb(n, 0) comb(n, n) return 1. k > n, result 0. large inputs warn returning infinite result.

title: Base Utilities
text: Gene ID conversion GMT parsing compose naturally. example reads GMT file, converts gene symbols Entrez IDs, produces named list ID vectors ready enrichment analysis.
code: library(evanverse)  # 1. Parse GMT into long format tmp <- toy_gmt(n = 5) df  <- gmt2df(tmp)  # 2. Convert symbols to Entrez IDs ref    <- toy_gene_ref(species = "human") id_map <- gene2entrez(df$gene, ref = ref, species = "human")  # 3. Attach IDs and drop unmatched df$entrez_id <- id_map$entrez_id df <- df[!is.na(df$entrez_id), ]  # 4. Rebuild named list with Entrez IDs gs_entrez <- df2list(df, group_col = "term", value_col = "entrez_id") gs_entrez[["HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY"]] #> [1] "7157" "672"  "4609" "1956" "5728" "1031" "4193" "5925" "1029"  "207"

title: Get Started
text: evanverse utility toolkit combines package management, data helpers, plotting tools, statistical workflows, convenient operators. Note: code examples static (eval = FALSE). copy-paste everything fan starts roaring, congratulations: laptop entered “research mode”.
code: library(evanverse)

title: Get Started
text: Try operators first:
code: "Good" %p% "morning" #> [1] "Good morning"  c("A", "B", "C") %nin% c("B", "D") #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  c("tp53", "egfr") %match% c("TP53", "MYC", "EGFR") #> [1] 1 3

title: Get Started
text: Configure mirrors set_mirror(), use pak installation:
code: # Set CRAN + Bioconductor mirrors (great for users in China) set_mirror("all", "tuna")  # pak handles CRAN, GitHub, and Bioconductor with a unified interface pak::pkg_install("dplyr") pak::pkg_install("tidyverse/ggplot2")   # GitHub pak::pkg_install("bioc::DESeq2")        # Bioconductor  # Check status or update pak::pkg_status(c("dplyr", "ggplot2")) pak::pkg_update()

title: Get Started
code: df <- data.frame(   group = c("A", "A", "B"),   gene  = c("TP53", "EGFR", "BRCA1"),   score = c(1.2, 0.8, 1.6) )  df2list(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "gene") #> $A #> [1] "TP53" "EGFR" #> $B #> [1] "BRCA1"  df2vect(df, name_col = "gene", value_col = "score") #>  TP53  EGFR BRCA1 #>   1.2   0.8   1.6

title: Get Started
code: path <- toy_gmt(n = 3)  gmt_df <- gmt2df(path) head(gmt_df, 3) #>                    term               description  gene #> 1 HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY   Genes regulated by p53  TP53 #> ...  gmt_list <- gmt2list(path) names(gmt_list) #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY" "HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING" "HALLMARK_HYPOXIA"

title: Get Started
text: Correlation summary:
code: set.seed(42) df <- data.frame(   group = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 30),   value = c(rnorm(30, 5), rnorm(30, 6)) )  res_t <- quick_ttest(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "value") print(res_t) #> t.test | p = ... res_cor <- quick_cor(mtcars) print(res_cor) #> pearson | ... vars | ... significant pairs

title: Get Started
code: plot_bar(   data = data.frame(category = c("A", "B", "C"), value = c(10, 7, 12)),   x_col = "category",   y_col = "value" ) #> # A ggplot object

title: Get Started
code: # 1) Prepare toy reference and gene sets ref  <- toy_gene_ref("human", n = 100) path <- toy_gmt(n = 3)  # 2) Parse GMT and convert gene IDs long <- gmt2df(path) idm  <- gene2entrez(long$gene, ref = ref, species = "human") long$entrez_id <- idm$entrez_id  # 3) Build list per term long2 <- long[!is.na(long$entrez_id), ] sets  <- df2list(long2, group_col = "term", value_col = "entrez_id")  # 4) Quick statistical and plotting step res <- quick_cor(mtcars[, c("mpg", "hp", "wt", "qsec")]) plot(res, type = "upper", show_sig = TRUE)

text: set_mirror() configures mirrors used R’s package system (respected pak). Supported mirror names: CRAN-mirrors used repo = "":
code: # Set both CRAN + Bioconductor mirrors (default mirror: tuna) set_mirror() #> v CRAN mirror set to: https://mirrors.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/CRAN #> v Bioconductor mirror set to: https://mirrors.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/bioconductor  # CRAN only set_mirror("cran", "westlake")  # Bioconductor only set_mirror("bioc", "official") set_mirror("all", "aliyun") #> Error in `set_mirror()`: #> ! Mirror "aliyun" is CRAN-only and cannot be used with repo = "all".

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: ops module provides five infix operators cover four common tasks: Note: code examples vignette static (eval = FALSE). Output hand-written reflect current implementation. modify operators, re-verify examples manually switch chunks eval = TRUE. operators accept character input validate arguments raise informative error non-character NA inputs. %match% %map% also reject empty strings. %nin% %% unrestricted — mirror base R behaviour type.
code: library(evanverse)

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Concatenates two character vectors element-wise single space. Equivalent paste(lhs, rhs, sep = " ") reads naturally pipelines. length-1 operand recycled longer vector usual R fashion: unequal lengths rejected rather recycled silently: Empty strings valid — space always inserted: NA values non-character inputs rejected:
code: "Hello" %p% "world" #> [1] "Hello world"  c("good", "hello") %p% c("morning", "world") #> [1] "good morning" "hello world" "Gene:" %p% c("TP53", "BRCA1", "MYC") #> [1] "Gene: TP53"  "Gene: BRCA1" "Gene: MYC" c("a", "b", "c") %p% c("x", "y") #> Error in `%p%()`: #> ! `lhs` and `rhs` must have equal lengths, or one side must have length 1. "" %p% "world" #> [1] " world" "Hello" %p% NA #> Error in `%p%()`: #> ! `rhs` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  123 %p% "world" #> Error in `%p%()`: #> ! `lhs` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Returns TRUE every element x present table. concise alternative !(x %% table). %nin% mirrors %% exactly — accepts type follows base R semantics NA type coercion: R coerces types comparing, character strings can match numeric values printed representation: Empty vectors return zero-length results without error:
code: c("A", "B", "C") %nin% c("B", "D") #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  1:5 %nin% c(2, 4) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE # NA matches NA in the table NA %nin% c(NA, 1) #> [1] FALSE  # NA does not match non-NA elements NA %nin% c(1, 2) #> [1] TRUE c("1", "2") %nin% c(1, 2) #> [1] FALSE FALSE c("a", "b") %nin% character(0)   # nothing to be in #> [1] TRUE TRUE  character(0) %nin% c("a", "b") #> logical(0)

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: %match% %map% lower-case sides comparing, "tp53" "TP53" treated string. require non-NA, non-empty character vectors sides, reject empty strings.

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Like base::match(), case-insensitive. Returns integer vector positions; unmatched elements become NA. table contains duplicates case normalisation, index first match returned warning: Duplicate elements x matched independently: Non-character inputs, NA values, empty vectors rejected sides:
code: c("tp53", "BRCA1", "egfr") %match% c("TP53", "EGFR", "MYC") #> [1]  1 NA  2 c("x") %match% c("X", "x", "X") #> Warning: `table` contains duplicated value after case normalization: "x". #> Using the first match. #> [1] 1 c("tp53", "tp53") %match% c("TP53", "EGFR") #> [1] 1 1 # empty x character(0) %match% c("TP53") #> Error in `%match%()`: #> ! `x` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # NA in x c("tp53", NA) %match% c("TP53") #> Error in `%match%()`: #> ! `x` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # empty table c("tp53") %match% character(0) #> Error in `%match%()`: #> ! `table` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # NA in table c("tp53") %match% c("TP53", NA) #> Error in `%match%()`: #> ! `table` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # empty string in x c("tp53", "") %match% c("TP53") #> Error in `%match%()`: #> ! `x` must not contain NA or empty string values.

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Like %match%, returns named character vector instead indices. Names canonical entries table; values original elements x. Unmatched entries silently dropped. Output order follows x. Output order follows x, table: Unmatched elements dropped rather returned NA: nothing matches, empty named character vector returned: Duplicate elements x match retained: Duplicate values table case normalisation warn use first canonical entry: error rules %match% apply sides:
code: c("tp53", "brca1", "egfr") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR", "MYC") #>   TP53   EGFR #> "tp53" "egfr" c("egfr", "tp53") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR") #>   EGFR   TP53 #> "egfr" "tp53" c("akt1", "tp53") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR") #>   TP53 #> "tp53" c("none1", "none2") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR") #> named character(0) c("tp53", "tp53") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR") #>   TP53   TP53 #> "tp53" "tp53" c("tp53") %map% c("TP53", "tp53") #> Warning: `table` contains duplicated value after case normalization: "tp53". #> Using the first match. #>   TP53 #> "tp53" # empty x character(0) %map% c("TP53") #> Error in `%map%()`: #> ! `x` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # NA in x c("tp53", NA) %map% c("TP53") #> Error in `%map%()`: #> ! `x` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # empty table c("tp53") %map% character(0) #> Error in `%map%()`: #> ! `table` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # NA in table c("tp53") %map% c("TP53", NA) #> Error in `%map%()`: #> ! `table` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # empty string in table c("tp53") %map% c("TP53", "") #> Error in `%map%()`: #> ! `table` must not contain NA or empty string values.

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Wraps base::identical(). Returns single TRUE FALSE tolerance type attribute differences. Unlike ==, %% distinguishes types, names, storage mode: NULL NA variants handled correctly: %% accepts type — input restriction.
code: 1:3 %is% 1:3 #> [1] TRUE  "hello" %is% "hello" #> [1] TRUE  list(a = 1) %is% list(a = 1) #> [1] TRUE 1:3 %is% c(1, 2, 3)          # integer vs double #> [1] FALSE  c(a = 1, b = 2) %is% c(b = 1, a = 2)   # same values, different names #> [1] FALSE NULL %is% NULL #> [1] TRUE  NA %is% NA #> [1] TRUE  NA %is% NA_real_   # logical NA vs double NA #> [1] FALSE

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: operators compose naturally bioinformatics pipelines. example filters gene table canonical symbols, maps aliases official form, labels gene’s match status.
code: library(evanverse)  canonical <- c("TP53", "BRCA1", "EGFR", "MYC", "PTEN") query     <- c("tp53", "brca1", "AKT1", "egfr", "unknown")  # 1. Which queries are not in the canonical set (case-insensitive)? missing_idx <- which(is.na(query %match% canonical)) query[missing_idx] #> [1] "AKT1"    "unknown"  # 2. Map matched queries to their canonical names query %map% canonical #>    TP53   BRCA1    EGFR #> "tp53" "brca1"  "egfr"  # 3. Build an annotation column using %p% anno <- "Gene:" %p% canonical anno #> [1] "Gene: TP53"  "Gene: BRCA1" "Gene: EGFR"  "Gene: MYC"   "Gene: PTEN"  # 4. Check that the canonical list hasn't changed canonical %is% c("TP53", "BRCA1", "EGFR", "MYC", "PTEN") #> [1] TRUE

title: Package Management
text: found pak provides elegant performant package management workflow. supports parallel installation, automatic dependency resolution, unified interface package sources — inst_pkg(), check_pkg(), update_pkg(), pkg_version() removed evanverse.
code: # Install pak install.packages("pak")  # Set a fast mirror first (evanverse) set_mirror("all", "tuna")  # Install from any source with a unified interface pak::pkg_install("ggplot2")                              # CRAN pak::pkg_install("tidyverse/ggplot2")                    # GitHub pak::pkg_install("bioc::DESeq2")                         # Bioconductor pak::pkg_install(c("dplyr", "bioc::limma", "r-lib/pak")) # mixed  # Check status pak::pkg_status(c("ggplot2", "DESeq2"))  # Update all or specific packages pak::pkg_update() pak::pkg_update("ggplot2")

title: Statistical Utilities
text: stat module provides six functions across five common statistical tasks: Note: code examples vignette static (eval = FALSE). Output hand-written reflect current implementation.
code: library(evanverse)

title: Statistical Utilities
text: stat_power() stat_n() support test families:

what: stat_power() - Compute power from sample size
title: Statistical Utilities
text: Given n, effect_size, test settings, returns power_result object computed power plus interpretation recommendation. ANOVA, k required: chi-square, df required: Use S3 methods reporting visualization:
code: res <- stat_power(n = 30, effect_size = 0.5) print(res) #> Power: 47.8%  (very low) | Two-sample t-test #> n = 30 per group,  effect size = 0.500,  alpha = 0.050 stat_power(n = 25, effect_size = 0.25, test = "anova", k = 3) #> # power_result object stat_power(n = 120, effect_size = 0.3, test = "chisq", df = 2) #> # power_result object summary(res) plot(res) #> # A ggplot object: power curve over sample size

title: Statistical Utilities
text: Given target power expected effect_size, returns minimum required sample size (rounded ) power_result object. One-sided correlation example: Use S3 methods stat_power():
code: res_n <- stat_n(power = 0.8, effect_size = 0.5) print(res_n) #> n = 64 per group (128 total) | Two-sample t-test #> Target power = 80%,  effect size = 0.500,  alpha = 0.050 stat_n(   power = 0.9,   effect_size = 0.3,   test = "correlation",   alternative = "greater" ) #> # power_result object summary(res_n) plot(res_n) #> # A ggplot object: required n as a function of effect size

title: Statistical Utilities
text: quick_ttest() compares exactly two groups numeric outcome. method = "auto", chooses t.test wilcox.test based normality checks sample-size-aware rules. Paired comparison requires paired = TRUE id_col:
code: set.seed(42) df <- data.frame(   group = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 30),   value = c(rnorm(30, 5), rnorm(30, 6)) )  res_t <- quick_ttest(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "value") print(res_t) #> t.test | p = 0.0012* | A n=30, B n=30 summary(res_t) set.seed(7) df_p <- data.frame(   id    = rep(1:20, 2),   group = rep(c("pre", "post"), each = 20),   value = c(rnorm(20, 10, 2), rnorm(20, 11, 2)) )  res_p <- quick_ttest(   df_p,   group_col = "group",   value_col = "value",   paired = TRUE,   id_col = "id" ) plot(res_p, plot_type = "both", p_label = "p.format")

title: Statistical Utilities
text: quick_anova() compares 2+ groups numeric outcome performs assumption diagnostics. Auto-selection logic: Check group normality. normality passes, choose ANOVA vs Welch variance equality. normality fails, use Kruskal-Wallis. can force method post-hoc behavior explicitly:
code: set.seed(123) df_a <- data.frame(   group = rep(LETTERS[1:3], each = 40),   value = rnorm(120, mean = rep(c(0, 0.5, 1.2), each = 40), sd = 1) )  res_a <- quick_anova(df_a, group_col = "group", value_col = "value") print(res_a) #> One-way ANOVA | p < 0.001* | A n=40, B n=40, C n=40 summary(res_a) res_a2 <- quick_anova(   df_a,   group_col = "group",   value_col = "value",   method = "anova",   post_hoc = "tukey" ) plot(res_a2, plot_type = "violin")

title: Statistical Utilities
text: quick_chisq() tests association two categorical variables. method = "auto", method choice depends table shape expected frequencies. 2x2 small-sample Fisher example: method = "mcnemar" paired/matched categorical outcomes .
code: set.seed(123) df_c <- data.frame(   treatment = sample(c("A", "B", "C"), 100, replace = TRUE),   response  = sample(c("Success", "Failure"), 100, replace = TRUE,                      prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) )  res_c <- quick_chisq(df_c, x_col = "treatment", y_col = "response") print(res_c) #> Chi-square test | p = 0.0410* | 3x2 | V = 0.20 (small) summary(res_c) df_2x2 <- data.frame(   exposed = c("Yes", "Yes", "No", "No", "Yes", "No"),   event   = c("Yes", "No", "Yes", "No", "Yes", "No") )  res_f <- quick_chisq(df_2x2, x_col = "exposed", y_col = "event", method = "fisher") plot(res_f, plot_type = "heatmap")

what: quick_cor() - Correlation matrix with significance summary
title: Statistical Utilities
text: quick_cor() computes pairwise correlations, p-values, optional multiple-testing adjustment, returns heatmap-ready result object. Restrict variables use BH-adjusted p-values: p_adjust_method != "none", significance based adjusted p-values.
code: res_cor <- quick_cor(mtcars) print(res_cor) #> pearson | 11 vars | 24/55 significant pairs (alpha = 0.05) summary(res_cor) res_cor2 <- quick_cor(   mtcars,   vars = c("mpg", "hp", "wt", "qsec"),   method = "spearman",   p_adjust_method = "BH" ) plot(res_cor2, type = "upper", show_sig = TRUE)

title: Statistical Utilities
text: planning analysis functions compose naturally practice:
code: library(evanverse)  # 1. Plan sample size plan <- stat_n(power = 0.8, effect_size = 0.5, test = "t_two")  # 2. Collect / prepare data and run a primary two-group test set.seed(101) df <- data.frame(   group = rep(c("Control", "Treatment"), each = plan$n),   value = c(rnorm(plan$n, 10, 2), rnorm(plan$n, 11, 2)) ) res_t <- quick_ttest(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "value")  # 3. Explore correlation structure among related numeric variables res_c <- quick_cor(mtcars[, c("mpg", "disp", "hp", "wt", "qsec")])  # 4. Report + visualize print(res_t) plot(res_t) print(res_c) plot(res_c, type = "upper", show_sig = TRUE)

what: toy_gene_ref() - Generate a compact gene reference table
title: Toy Data Utilities
text: toy_gene_ref() returns small deterministic reference table compatible gene2entrez() / gene2ensembl() workflows. human reference includes symbols used toy_gmt(), GMT parsing gene ID conversion examples can run offline together. Key behavior: species supports "human" "mouse". n controls returned rows capped 100 available rows. Symbols Ensembl IDs unique within species. Mouse example: n larger 100 silently capped: Invalid n (0, negative, non-integer) raises error.
code: ref_human <- toy_gene_ref() head(ref_human, 3) #>          symbol       ensembl_id entrez_id      gene_type species ensembl_version download_date #> 1          TP53 ENSG00000141510      7157 protein_coding   human             113    2025-04-23 #> 2         BRCA1 ENSG00000012048       672         lncRNA   human             113    2025-04-23 #> 3           MYC ENSG00000136997      4609     pseudogene   human             113    2025-04-23 ref_mouse <- toy_gene_ref(species = "mouse", n = 10) ref_mouse[, c("symbol", "ensembl_id", "species")] #>          symbol          ensembl_id species #> 1         Trp53 ENSMUSG00000059552   mouse #> 2         Brca1 ENSMUSG00000017146   mouse #> ... nrow(toy_gene_ref(n = 999)) #> [1] 100

what: toy_gmt() - Write a temporary GMT file
title: Toy Data Utilities
text: toy_gmt() writes GMT file temporary path returns file path. designed feed directly gmt2df() gmt2list(). Key behavior: Default n = 5 writes 5 gene sets. n capped 5 available built-sets. Every line GMT-formatted: term, description, genes. Invalid n (0, negative, non-integer) raises error.
code: path <- toy_gmt() path #> [1] "C:/Users/.../Rtmp.../file....gmt"  readLines(path)[1] #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY\tGenes regulated by p53\tTP53\tBRCA1\tMYC\tEGFR\t..." length(readLines(toy_gmt(n = 1))) #> [1] 1  length(readLines(toy_gmt(n = 3))) #> [1] 3  length(readLines(toy_gmt(n = 99))) #> [1] 5

title: Toy Data Utilities
text: common offline testing flow : Build small reference toy_gene_ref(). Build small gene-set file toy_gmt(). Parse GMT convert symbols IDs using base converters.
code: library(evanverse)  # 1. Toy reference ref <- toy_gene_ref(species = "human", n = 100)  # 2. Toy gene sets path <- toy_gmt(n = 3) long <- gmt2df(path)  # 3. Convert symbols to Entrez IDs id_map <- gene2entrez(long$gene, ref = ref, species = "human") long$entrez_id <- id_map$entrez_id  # 4. Rebuild list of Entrez IDs per term long2 <- long[!is.na(long$entrez_id), ] sets_entrez <- df2list(long2, group_col = "term", value_col = "entrez_id")  names(sets_entrez) #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY" "HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING" "HALLMARK_HYPOXIA"

what: Number of combinations C(n, k) — comb
title: Number of combinations C(n, k) — comb
text: Calculates number ways choose k items n distinct items (unordered). C(n, k) = n! / (k! * (n - k)!)

text: Produces publication-ready forest plot UKB-standard styling. user supplies data frame whose first column row label (item), plus additional display columns (e.g. Cases/N). gap column auto-formatted (95% CI) text column inserted automatically ci_column. Numeric p-value columns declared via p_cols formatted -place.

code: plot_forest(   data,   est,   lower,   upper,   ci_column = 2L,   ref_line = 1,   xlim = NULL,   ticks_at = NULL,   arrow_lab = c("Lower risk", "Higher risk"),   header = NULL,   indent = NULL,   bold_label = NULL,   ci_col = "black",   ci_sizes = 0.6,   ci_Theight = 0.2,   ci_digits = 2L,   ci_sep = ", ",   p_cols = NULL,   p_digits = 3L,   bold_p = TRUE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   align = NULL,   background = "zebra",   bg_col = "#F0F0F0",   border = "three_line",   border_width = 3,   row_height = NULL,   col_width = NULL,   save = FALSE,   dest = NULL,   save_width = 20,   save_height = NULL,   theme = "default" )

title: Publication-ready forest plot — plot_forest
text: data data.frame. First column must label column (item). Additional columns displayed -(character) formatted named p_cols (must numeric). Column order preserved. est Numeric vector. Point estimates (NA = CI drawn). lower Numeric vector. Lower CI bounds (length est). upper Numeric vector. Upper CI bounds (length est). ci_column Integer. Column position final rendered table gap/CI graphic placed. Must 2 ncol(data) + 1 (inclusive). Default: 2L. ref_line Numeric. Reference line. Default: 1 (HR/). Use 0 beta coefficients. xlim Numeric vector length 2. X-axis limits. NULL (default) uses c(0, 2). ticks_at Numeric vector. Tick positions. NULL (default) = 5 evenly spaced ticks across xlim. arrow_lab Character vector length 2. Directional labels. Default: c("Lower risk", "Higher risk"). NULL = none. header Character vector length ncol(data) + 2. Column header labels final rendered table (original columns + gap_ci + label). NULL (default) = use column names data plus "gap_ci" "(95% CI)". Pass "" gap column position. indent Integer vector (length = nrow(data)). Indentation level label column: unit adds two leading spaces. Default: zeros. bold_label Logical vector (length = nrow(data)). rows bold label column. NULL (default) = auto-derive indent: rows indent == 0 bolded (parent rows), indented sub-rows plain. ci_col Character scalar vector (length = nrow(data)). CI colour(s). NA rows skipped automatically. Default: "black". ci_sizes Numeric. Point size. Default: 0.6. ci_Theight Numeric. CI cap height. Default: 0.2. ci_digits Integer. Decimal places auto-generated (95% CI) column. Default: 2L. ci_sep Character. Separator lower upper CI label, e.g. ", " " - ". Default: ", ". p_cols Character vector. Names numeric p-value columns data. formatted p_digits decimal places "<0.001"-style clipping. NULL = none. p_digits Integer. Decimal places p-value formatting. Default: 3L. bold_p TRUE (bold non-NA p p_threshold), FALSE (bolding), logical vector (per-row control). Default: TRUE. p_threshold Numeric. P-value threshold bolding bold_p = TRUE. Default: 0.05. align Integer vector length ncol(data) + 2. Alignment per column: -1 left, 0 centre, 1 right. Must cover final columns (original + gap_ci + label). NULL = auto (column 1 left, others centre). background Character. Row background style: "zebra", "bold_label" (shade rows bold_label = TRUE), "none". Default: "zebra". bg_col Character. Shading colour backgrounds (scalar), per-row vector length nrow(data) (overrides style). Default: "#F0F0F0". border Character. Border style: "three_line" "none". Default: "three_line". border_width Numeric. Border line width(s). Scalar = three lines width; length-3 vector = top--header, bottom--header, bottom--body. Default: 3. row_height NULL (auto), numeric scalar, numeric vector (length = total gtable rows including margins). Auto sets 8 / 12 / 10 / 15 mm top / header / data / bottom respectively. col_width NULL (auto), numeric scalar, numeric vector (length = total gtable columns). Auto rounds default width adjustment \([5, 10)\) mm. save Logical. Save output files? Default: FALSE. dest Character. Destination file path (extension ignored; PNG, PDF, JPG, TIFF files saved). Required save = TRUE. save_width Numeric. Output width cm. Default: 20. save_height Numeric NULL. Output height cm. NULL = nrow(data) * 0.9 + 3. theme Character preset ("default") forestploter::forest_theme object. Default: "default".

title: One-way comparison with automatic method selection — quick_anova
text: Auto method selection logic: Normality checked per group via Shapiro-Wilk (sample-size-adaptive thresholds — see quick_ttest details). normality passes: Levene's test decides classical ANOVA (equal variances) Welch ANOVA (unequal variances). normality fails: Kruskal-Wallis used. Post-hoc defaults post_hoc = "auto": "anova" → Tukey HSD "welch" → Pairwise Welch t-tests (BH-adjusted) "kruskal" → Pairwise Wilcoxon (BH-adjusted)

title: One-way comparison with automatic method selection — quick_anova
code: set.seed(123) df <- data.frame(   group = rep(LETTERS[1:3], each = 40),   value = rnorm(120, mean = rep(c(0, 0.5, 1.2), each = 40), sd = 1) ) result <- quick_anova(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "value") print(result) #> One-way ANOVA | p < 0.001* | A n=40, B n=40, C n=40 summary(result) #>  #> ── One-way Comparison ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Parameters ── #>  #> Test: One-way ANOVA #> alpha: 0.050 #>  #> ── Omnibus Test ── #>  #> ✔ p < 0.001  (significant at alpha = 0.05) #> eta_squared = 0.224, omega_squared = 0.210 #>  #>              Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value   Pr(>F)     #> group         2  27.51  13.755   16.91 3.53e-07 *** #> Residuals   117  95.14   0.813                      #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> ── Descriptive statistics ── #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 7 #>   group     n   mean    sd median    min   max #>   <fct> <int>  <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 A        40 0.0452 0.898 0.0906 -1.97   1.79 #> 2 B        40 0.493  0.960 0.437  -1.81   2.67 #> 3 C        40 1.21   0.844 1.15   -0.468  3.39 #> ── Normality (Shapiro-Wilk) ── #>  #> A: n = 40, p = 0.953 #> B: n = 40, p = 0.940 #> C: n = 40, p = 0.913 #> -> Medium samples (min n = 40). Data reasonably normal (all Shapiro p ≥ 0.01). #>  #> ── Variance (Levene's test) ── #>  #> p = 0.854 | equal variances: TRUE #>  #> ── Post-hoc (tukey) ── #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   group2 group1  diff     lwr   upr     `p adj` #>   <chr>  <chr>  <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1 B      A      0.448 -0.0306 0.927 0.0716      #> 2 C      A      1.16   0.684  1.64  0.000000201 #> 3 C      B      0.715  0.236  1.19  0.00163     #>  plot(result) #> ! title: Categorical association test with automatic method selection — quick_chisq
text: Auto method selection logic: 2×2 table expected frequency < 5: Fisher's exact test >20\ 2x2 table 5 <= expected frequency < 10: Yates' correction applied Otherwise: standard chi-square test WARNING: "mcnemar" paired/matched data (e.g., -measurements subjects). use independent samples — use "chisq" "fisher" instead.

title: Categorical association test with automatic method selection — quick_chisq
code: set.seed(123) df <- data.frame(   treatment = sample(c("A", "B", "C"), 100, replace = TRUE),   response  = sample(c("Success", "Failure"), 100, replace = TRUE,                      prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) ) result <- quick_chisq(df, x_col = "treatment", y_col = "response") print(result) #> Chi-square test | p = 0.9568 | 3x2 | V = 0.03 (negligible) summary(result) #>  #> ── Categorical Association Test ──────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Parameters ── #>  #> Test: Chi-square test #> Variables: treatment × response #> Table size: 3x2 #> alpha: 0.050 #>  #> ── Result ── #>  #> ℹ p = 0.9568  (not significant at alpha = 0.05) #>  #>  #> 	Pearson's Chi-squared test #>  #> data:  cont_table #> X-squared = 0.088413, df = 2, p-value = 0.9568 #>  #>  #> ── Effect Size (Cramer's V) ── #>  #> V: 0.03 #> Interpretation: negligible #>  #> ── Observed Frequencies ── #>  #>     #>     Failure Success #>   A      13      20 #>   B      13      19 #>   C      13      22 #> ── Expected Frequencies ── #>  #>   Failure Success #> A   12.87   20.13 #> B   12.48   19.52 #> C   13.65   21.35 #> ── Pearson Residuals ── #>  #>     #>     Failure Success #>   A    0.04   -0.03 #>   B    0.15   -0.12 #>   C   -0.18    0.14 #> → |residual| > 2 indicates significant deviation from independence #>  #> ── Method Selection ── #>  #> Table size: 3x2 #> Total N: 100 #> Min expected freq: 12.48 #> Cells with freq < 5: 0 #> Decision: All expected frequencies adequate: using standard chi-square test #>  plot(result) #> ! code: set.seed(42) df <- data.frame(   group = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 30),   value = c(rnorm(30, 5), rnorm(30, 6)) ) result <- quick_ttest(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "value") print(result) #> t.test | p = 0.0089* | A n=30, B n=30 summary(result) #>  #> ── Two-group Comparison ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Parameters ── #>  #> Test: Welch two-sample t-test #> Direction: A - B #> Alternative: two.sided #> alpha: 0.050 #> Paired: FALSE #>  #> ── Result ── #>  #> ✔ p = 0.0089  (significant at alpha = 0.05) #>  #>  #> 	Welch Two Sample t-test #>  #> data:  value by group #> t = -2.7096, df = 56.249, p-value = 0.008913 #> alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group A and group B is not equal to 0 #> 95 percent confidence interval: #>  -1.4079331 -0.2110762 #> sample estimates: #> mean in group A mean in group B  #>        5.068587        5.878091  #>  #>  #> ── Descriptive statistics ── #>  #> # A tibble: 2 × 7 #>   group     n  mean    sd median   min   max #>   <fct> <int> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 A        30  5.07  1.26   4.90  2.34  7.29 #> 2 B        30  5.88  1.05   6.15  3.01  7.58 #> ── Normality (Shapiro-Wilk) ── #>  #> A: n = 30, p = 0.350 #> B: n = 30, p = 0.064 #> → Medium samples (min n = 30). Data reasonably normal (all Shapiro p ≥ 0.01). #>  plot(result) #> ! Could not load palette "qual_vivid". Using ggplot2 defaults.

title: Calculate Required Sample Size — stat_n
text: Computes minimum sample size needed achieve target statistical power detecting given effect size. Supports t-tests (two-sample, one-sample, paired), ANOVA, proportion tests, correlation tests, chi-square tests.

title: Calculate Required Sample Size — stat_n
code: stat_n(   power = 0.8,   effect_size,   test = c("t_two", "t_one", "t_paired", "anova", "proportion", "correlation", "chisq"),   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   alpha = 0.05,   k = NULL,   df = NULL )

title: Calculate Required Sample Size — stat_n
text: power Numeric. Target statistical power. Default: 0.8. effect_size Numeric. Effect size appropriate chosen test (must positive). Conventions: Cohen's d t-tests (small 0.2, medium 0.5, large 0.8) Cohen's f ANOVA (small 0.1, medium 0.25, large 0.4) Cohen's h proportion tests (small 0.2, medium 0.5, large 0.8) Pearson r correlation (small 0.1, medium 0.3, large 0.5) Cohen's w chi-square (small 0.1, medium 0.3, large 0.5) test Character. Test type: "t_two" (two-sample t-test, default), "t_one" (one-sample t-test), "t_paired" (paired t-test), "anova", "proportion", "correlation", "chisq". alternative Character. Direction alternative hypothesis: "two.sided" (default), "less", "greater". Ignored "anova" "chisq". alpha Numeric. Significance level (Type error rate). Default: 0.05. k Integer \(\ge 2\). Number groups. Required test = "anova". df Integer \(\ge 1\). Degrees freedom. Required test = "chisq".

title: Calculate Required Sample Size — stat_n
text: object class "power_result" (invisibly) containing: params Named list input parameters n Required sample size, rounded . Per group "t_two" "anova"; number pairs "t_paired"; total others. computed "n" — distinguishes stat_power() results computed = "power" interpretation Plain-text interpretation recommendation Practical recruitment advice Use print(result) brief summary, summary(result) full details, plot(result) display sample size curve.

title: Calculate Required Sample Size — stat_n
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Two-sample t-test (default) result <- stat_n(power = 0.8, effect_size = 0.5) print(result) summary(result) plot(result)  # ANOVA with 4 groups, higher power target stat_n(power = 0.9, effect_size = 0.25, test = "anova", k = 4)  # Correlation, one-sided stat_n(power = 0.8, effect_size = 0.3, test = "correlation",        alternative = "greater") } # }

title: Calculate Statistical Power — stat_power
text: n Integer. Sample size. Interpretation depends test: "t_two", "anova": per group "t_paired": number pairs others: total effect_size Numeric. Effect size appropriate chosen test (must positive). Conventions: Cohen's d t-tests (small 0.2, medium 0.5, large 0.8) Cohen's f ANOVA (small 0.1, medium 0.25, large 0.4) Cohen's h proportion tests (small 0.2, medium 0.5, large 0.8) Pearson r correlation (small 0.1, medium 0.3, large 0.5) Cohen's w chi-square (small 0.1, medium 0.3, large 0.5) test Character. Test type: "t_two" (two-sample t-test, default), "t_one" (one-sample t-test), "t_paired" (paired t-test), "anova", "proportion", "correlation", "chisq". alternative Character. Direction alternative hypothesis: "two.sided" (default), "less", "greater". Ignored "anova" "chisq". alpha Numeric. Significance level (Type error rate). Default: 0.05. k Integer \(\ge 2\). Number groups. Required test = "anova". df Integer \(\ge 1\). Degrees freedom. Required test = "chisq". x power_result object returned stat_power() stat_n(). y Ignored. plot_range Numeric vector length 2. code: # OLD (0.3.6) - No longer works in 0.3.7 create_palette("my_palette", "sequential", colors)  # NEW (0.3.7) - Explicit path required temp_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "palettes") create_palette("my_palette", "sequential", colors, color_dir = temp_dir)  # For production use, specify your desired directory create_palette("my_palette", "sequential", colors,                color_dir = "path/to/your/palette/directory")

text: focused refactor release: unified CLI messaging, CRAN-safe startup, tighter parameter validation, stronger tests, several new utilities.

title: evanverse 0.3.0
text: plot_forest() — Forest plots publication-style defaults (forestploter), significance bolding, color recycling, alignment options. plot_bar() — Bar charts optional fill grouping, sorting, vertical/horizontal layout, clean defaults. read_excel_flex() — Enhanced Excel reader (readxl) optional name cleaning (janitor), range/col_types controls, CLI feedback. write_xlsx_flex() — Flexible Excel writer (openxlsx) header styling, auto column width, overwrite/timestamp options. view() — Quick interactive data viewer (reactable) exploration/QC. set_mirror() — Switch CRAN/Bioconductor mirrors predefined, named endpoints. pkg_functions() — List exported functions installed package, optional keyword filtering.

title: evanverse 0.3.0
text: Inlined, robust delimiter detection .csv/.tsv/.txt .gz files Stricter parameter checks clearer CLI error messages Enhanced encoding guardrails better file handling Source normalization via strict match() function Supports “CRAN”, “GitHub”, “Bioconductor” sources Clearer messages mirror handling aligned set_mirror() robust CRAN/Bioconductor database retrieval Helpful suggestions GitHub package installations Optional preview printing clearer CLI output %% %near% messages standardized (emoji) Tighter type/shape checks cleaner examples %nin% implemented Negate(%%) simple, predictable NA semantics map_column() uses unified cli output counts unmatched keys Skips numeric columns design, removes emoji, adds modular separators with_timer() simplified using tictoc timing cli output remind() rendering fixed cli braces partial/case-insensitive matches rgb2hex() input validation tightened concise CLI feedback rows_with_void() header streamlined, delegates detection is_void() .onAttach switched plain cli::cli_text() messages (emoji), CRAN-safe Roxygen examples trimmed non-interactive/CRAN environments Consistent headers separators throughout documentation

title: evanverse 0.3.0
